topic: A student learns their scholarship application was denied
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email sat glowing on the screen, a stark, unforgiving block of text. Liam reread the rejection letter for the prestigious astrophysics scholarship for the tenth time, his thumb hovering over the refresh button before pulling his hand back. A dull ache settled behind his eyes, a heavy pressure that made focusing on the complex equations in his textbook feel like trying to push water uphill. He paced the narrow confines of his dorm room, the worn carpet muffling the frantic rhythm of his footsteps. Every time he stopped, he ran a hand through his already disheveled hair, the gesture jerky and uncontrolled. He stared at his reflection in the darkened window—a pale, drawn face etched with a deep, unsettling stillness. The silence of the room amplified the frantic drumming in his chest.

[story 2]

"No," Maya whispered, the sound barely escaping her throat as she stared at the printed notification on the desk. The crisp white paper seemed to mock her with its finality. Her jaw tightened so fiercely she could feel the muscles vibrating beneath her skin. She picked up a pen, but the tip trembled, refusing to make contact with the sheet. Her breath hitched, coming in short, shallow gasps. She squeezed her eyes shut, trying to force the churning sensation in her stomach down, but it only churned harder. She stood up abruptly, knocking a stack of notebooks to the floor with a loud thud. Her movements were jerky, uncoordinated, as she began to pace the small space between her bed and the door, her knuckles white where she gripped the edge of the desk.

[story 3]

He kept replaying the interview in his mind, searching for the moment he had faltered, the precise sentence that had signaled his inadequacy. The air in the library felt thick, suffocating, pressing down on his shoulders. Every time a new notification popped up on his laptop—a reminder, a social media update—it was like a tiny shard of glass digging into his skin. He rubbed his temples, the skin tender and hot beneath his fingertips. He leaned back in his chair, the cheap plastic groaning in protest, and let out a slow, ragged exhale that didn't quite reach his lungs. His gaze drifted to the window, focusing on a distant, indifferent tree, trying to anchor himself in something solid amidst the swirling chaos inside his head.
